description: Frequently used adjectives for describing things

examples: Tämä on hyvä kirja. — This is a good book.
Hyvää päivää! — Good day!
Sää on huono tänään. — The weather is bad today.
Huono idea! — Bad idea!
Talo on iso. — The house is big.
Hänellä on iso koira. — He/she has a big dog.
Kissa on pieni. — The cat is small.
Pieni hetki, kiitos. — One small moment, please.
Tie on pitkä. — The road is long.
Hän on pitkä mies. — He is a tall man.
Matka on lyhyt. — The journey is short.
Hänellä on lyhyet hiukset. — He/she has short hair.
Minulla on uusi auto. — I have a new car.
Uusi vuosi alkaa tammikuussa. — The new year starts in January.
Tämä kirkko on vanha. — This church is old.
Vanha ystävä on paras ystävä. — An old friend is the best friend.
Hän on nuori nainen. — She is a young woman.
Nuoret oppivat nopeasti. — Young people learn fast.
Juna on nopea. — The train is fast.
Nopea vastaus, kiitos. — A quick answer, please.

items.notes: Stem changes: hidas → hitaa- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: hidasta, genitive: hitaan).
examples: Bussi on hidas. — The bus is slow.
Puhu hitaasti, kiitos. — Speak slowly, please.
Tämä tehtävä on helppo. — This task is easy.
Suomi ei ole helppo kieli. — Finnish is not an easy language.
Tentti oli vaikea. — The exam was difficult.
Tämä on vaikea kysymys. — This is a difficult question.

items.notes: Stem changes: kaunis → kaunii- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: kaunista, genitive: kauniin).
examples: Suomi on kaunis maa. — Finland is a beautiful country.
Kaunis päivä! — Beautiful day!
Tuo rakennus on ruma. — That building is ugly.
Ruma ankanpoikanen on satu. — The ugly duckling is a fairy tale.

items.notes: Stem changes: suuri → suure- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: suurta, genitive: suuren). More formal than 'iso'.
examples: Se on suuri ongelma. — It is a big problem.
Kiitos, suuri kiitos! — Thanks, many thanks!
Tämä on tärkeä asia. — This is an important matter.
Terveys on tärkeää. — Health is important.
Peli on hauska. — The game is fun.
Hauska tutustua! — Nice to meet you!
Hän on mukava ihminen. — He/she is a nice person.
Mukava nähdä sinua! — Nice to see you!

items.notes: Informal synonym of 'mukava'. Very common in spoken Finnish, especially among younger speakers.
examples: Se oli kiva ilta. — It was a nice evening.
Kiva kun tulit! — Nice that you came!

items.notes: Compound adjective: 'hyvän' (good, genitive) + 'näköinen' (looking). Follows -inen declension pattern. Used for people.
examples: Hän on hyvännäköinen mies. — He is a good-looking man.
Hyvännäköinen tarjoilija hymyili. — The good-looking waiter smiled.
Kahvi on vahvaa. — The coffee is strong.
Hän on vahva nainen. — She is a strong woman.

items.notes: Stem changes: heikko → heiko- in some forms due to consonant gradation (kk → k). Opposite of 'vahva'.
examples: Signaali on heikko. — The signal is weak.
Tee on liian heikkoa. — The tea is too weak.

items.notes: Used for objects (thick book, thick wall). For people, 'lihava' is more common for 'fat'. Partitive: paksua.
examples: Kirja on paksu. — The book is thick.
Jää on paksua. — The ice is thick.

items.notes: Stem changes: ohut → ohue- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: ohutta, genitive: ohuen). Opposite of 'paksu'.
examples: Jää on ohutta. — The ice is thin.
Tämä kangas on ohut. — This fabric is thin.

items.notes: Note the front vowels (e, ä). Partitive: leveää. Often used for roads, rivers, and shoulders.
examples: Joki on leveä. — The river is wide.
Tie on leveä ja suora. — The road is wide and straight.

items.notes: Opposite of 'leveä'. Partitive: kapeaa. Common in describing streets, passages, and physical shapes.
examples: Katu on kapea. — The street is narrow.
Kapea polku vie metsään. — A narrow path leads to the forest.

items.notes: Refers to weight, not brightness. Stem changes: kevyt → kevye- (e.g. partitive: kevyttä, genitive: kevyen). Also used for light food/meals.
examples: Laukku on kevyt. — The bag is light.
Haluaisin kevyen lounaan. — I would like a light lunch.

items.notes: Stem changes: raskas → raskaa- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: raskasta, genitive: raskaan). Also used figuratively: 'raskas päivä' (a heavy/tough day).
examples: Laatikko on raskas. — The box is heavy.
Työ on raskasta. — The work is heavy/hard.

items.notes: Note the front vowels (e, ä). Partitive: pehmeää. Used for texture, materials, and figuratively for personality.
examples: Tyyny on pehmeä. — The pillow is soft.
Vauvan iho on pehmeä. — The baby's skin is soft.
Kivi on kova. — The stone is hard.
Musiikki on liian kovalla. — The music is too loud.

items.notes: Note the front vowels (y, ä). Partitive: tyhjää. Can also be used figuratively: 'tyhjä lupaus' (an empty promise).
examples: Lasi on tyhjä. — The glass is empty.
Huone on tyhjä. — The room is empty.

items.notes: Stem changes: täysi → täyde- in some inflected forms (e.g. partitive: täyttä, genitive: täyden). Also 'täynnä' means 'full of'.
examples: Bussi on täynnä. — The bus is full.
Lasi on täysi. — The glass is full.

items.notes: Stem changes: puhdas → puhtaa- in inflected forms (e.g. partitive: puhdasta, genitive: puhtaan). Consonant gradation: hd → ht.
examples: Vesi on puhdasta. — The water is clean.
Ilma on puhdasta Suomessa. — The air is pure in Finland.

items.notes: Derived from 'lika' (dirt). Follows -inen adjective declension: likainen → likaista (partitive), likaisen (genitive).
examples: Vaatteet ovat likaiset. — The clothes are dirty.
Älä koske, se on likainen! — Don't touch, it is dirty!

items.notes: Stem changes due to consonant gradation: märkä → märä- (rk → r) in some forms. Partitive: märkää.
examples: Lattia on märkä. — The floor is wet.
Älä astu märälle lattialle! — Don't step on the wet floor!

items.notes: Opposite of 'märkä'. Partitive: kuivaa. Also used figuratively: 'kuiva huumori' (dry humor).
examples: Pyyhkeet ovat kuivat. — The towels are dry.
Kesä oli kuiva. — The summer was dry.

items.notes: Partitive form of 'tosi' (true/real). Used predicatively: 'Se on totta' (It is true). 'Tosi' is also used as an informal intensifier meaning 'really'.
examples: Se on totta! — That is true!
Onko se totta? — Is it true?

items.notes: Derived from 'mahto' (might/power). Follows -inen declension: mahdollista (partitive), mahdollisen (genitive). Often used with 'on': 'Se on mahdollista' (It is possible).
examples: Kaikki on mahdollista. — Everything is possible.
Se ei ole mahdollista. — It is not possible.

items.notes: Negative form of 'mahdollinen', using the -ton/-tön suffix which indicates absence. Stem: mahdottoma- (e.g. partitive: mahdotonta). The double 't' is critical.
examples: Tehtävä on mahdoton. — The task is impossible.
Se tuntuu mahdottomalta. — It feels impossible.

items.notes: Derived from 'tapa' (habit/way). Follows -inen declension. 'Tavallisesti' is the adverb form meaning 'usually'.
examples: Se on tavallinen päivä. — It is an ordinary day.
Tämä on tavallista. — This is normal/usual.

items.notes: Derived from 'eri' (different/separate). Follows -inen declension. Can have positive or neutral connotation, unlike 'outo' (strange/weird).
examples: Tämä on erikoinen tapaus. — This is a special case.
Ruoka oli erikoista. — The food was unusual.

items.notes: Compound: 'saman' (same, genitive) + 'lainen' (kind of). Follows -inen declension. Takes 'kuin' for comparison: 'samanlainen kuin sinä' (similar to you).
examples: Meillä on samanlaiset kengät. — We have similar shoes.
Tämä on samanlainen kuin edellinen. — This one is similar to the previous one.

items.notes: Compound: 'eri' (different) + 'lainen' (kind of). Follows -inen declension. Takes 'kuin' for comparison: 'erilainen kuin muut' (different from others).
examples: Jokainen ihminen on erilainen. — Every person is different.
Tämä on erilainen kuin tuo. — This is different from that.

items.notes: Derived from 'viime' (last, as in most recent). Follows -inen declension. 'Viime' is used for time (viime viikolla = last week), 'viimeinen' for sequences (viimeinen sivu = the last page).
examples: Tämä on viimeinen kysymys. — This is the last question.
Hän oli viimeinen jonossa. — He/she was the last in the queue.
